text: The patient is positioned in the low-lithotomy position on a thick egg crate with a tuck sheet. The surgeon pulls the patient down so the coccyx and ischial spines are at the lowest part of the bed, ensuring the buttocks and perineum are exposed. Both legs are placed in Allen stirrups with heels touching the back to offload the calf and prevent compartment syndrome. The legs are flexed at approximately 100 to 120 degrees, and the surgeon checks the heels to ensure no undue tension before fixing the stirrups in place.

The surgeon places foam or a sheet over the patient's chest at the level of the nipples and tapes the patient to the bed, ensuring a snug fit that allows for finger space between the foam and the chest. The Foley catheter is positioned over the patient's right leg, with visible padding and securement of the upper extremities. A thick foam pad spans from the neck to the buttocks, and the bed is tested in a steep Trendelenburg position to confirm the patient's secure placement without slippage.
